did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a classification method based on support vector machine (SVM) tools, combined with principal component analysis (PCA) and first-order statistics descriptors, to discriminate between melanomas and nevi in skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes the construction of an extended near-infrared multispectral imaging system using an InGaAs sensor, details a pilot study involving 39 nevi and 14 melanomas from patients, and reports original experimental results including sensitivity (78.6%) and specificity (84.6%) for discriminating between melanomas and nevi using SVM classification.

title: Visible and Extended Near-Infrared Multispectral Imaging for Skin Cancer Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study developed an extended near-infrared (exNIR) multispectral imaging system using an InGaAs sensor (995–1613 nm) to evaluate deeper skin layers for early, noninvasive skin cancer diagnosis. The system was combined with a visible and near-infrared (VIS-NIR) multispectral system (414–995 nm) to analyze spectral and spatial features from skin lesions. A classification method discriminating between melanomas and nevi was constructed using first-order statistics descriptors, principal component analysis (PCA), and support vector machine (SVM) tools. The approach was validated in a pilot study involving 39 nevi and 14 melanomas from patients at the Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona. The system achieved a sensitivity of 78.6% and a specificity of 84.6%, with the specificity being improved compared to previous silicon sensor-based systems. These results suggest that exNIR imaging provides valuable information for distinguishing between benign and malignant skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: 14 melanomas from Caucasian patients at the Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona were analyzed in the pilot study. They were diagnosed by dermatologists using a commercial dermoscope and the confocal laser scanning microscope VivaScope 1500. When malignancy was suspected, lesions were excised for histological analysis.
39 nevi from Caucasian patients at the Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona were analyzed in the pilot study. They served as the benign comparator class to discriminate against melanomas.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between melanomas (malignant) and nevi (benign)
all_task_types.details: The paper developed a classification method to discriminate between melanomas and nevi using first-order statistics descriptors, principal component analysis (PCA), and support vector machine (SVM) tools. The system achieved a sensitivity of 78.6% and a specificity of 84.6%.

datasets_used.name: Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona Pilot Study Cohort
datasets_used.description: A pilot study dataset consisting of skin lesion images from Caucasian patients at the Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain. Images were captured using a custom-built extended near-infrared (exNIR) multispectral imaging system (InGaAs sensor, 995–1613 nm) and a visible and near-infrared (VIS-NIR) multispectral system (LEDs, 414–995 nm). Lesions were diagnosed by dermatologists using a commercial dermoscope and confocal laser scanning microscope (VivaScope 1500), with histological analysis performed when malignancy was suspected.

use.training: The entire cohort was used for the development and evaluation of the classification method. The paper does not report a separate training/validation/testing split; the 53 lesions were analyzed collectively to derive the classification algorithm and report overall sensitivity and specificity.
use.other: The dataset was used to validate the performance of the exNIR and VIS-NIR multispectral imaging systems and the associated SVM classification algorithm. It served as the sole experimental dataset for reporting the system's sensitivity (78.6%) and specificity (84.6%).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract features from the histogram of segmented lesions for discrimination between benign and malignant tumors, including mean, maximum, minimum, standard deviation, energy, entropy, and third central moment (skewness).
To automatize and enhance the performance of the classification algorithm by reducing dimensionality and avoiding the use of manually selected experimental thresholds.
To construct a classification method to discriminate between melanomas and nevi based on the analyzed statistical descriptors and PCA components.
To standardize the data matrix for PCA by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation of every descriptor.

To determine the best statistical descriptors by experimentally setting upper and lower thresholds on scatter plots and calculating cumulative sensitivity as parameters were added to the classification model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity (78.6%) and specificity (84.6%) derived from a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classification algorithm and iterative thresholding on a pilot study cohort.

Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelon.dataset_description: Pilot study involving 39 nevi and 14 melanomas from Caucasian patients.
Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelon.classification_method: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and first-order statistics descriptors.
Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelon.notes: The specificity (84.6%) was improved with respect to that offered by silicon sensors. The system achieved a cumulative sensitivity of 100% with the iterative addition of 8 statistical descriptors before PCA/SVM application, but the final reported SVM classification results were 78.6% sensitivity and 84.6% specificity.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: First-order statistics descriptors (mean, maximum, minimum, standard deviation, energy, entropy, skewness) calculated from the histogram of segmented lesions in VIS-NIR (414–995 nm) and exNIR (995–1613 nm) multispectral images.
architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on standardized data matrix.
architecture_details.standardization: Subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation of every descriptor.
architecture_details.classifier: SVM used to find a hyperplane separating nevi and melanomas with maximum margin in the PCA-transformed space.

training_details.hyperparameters: Eight best statistical descriptors selected via iterative thresholding on scatter plots to maximize sensitivity; first three PCs used for classification.

classification_or_output_head.output: Decision boundary defined by support vectors; sensitivity 78.6%, specificity 84.6%.

preprocessing_techniques: Daily calibration using neutral reference (Neutral 5) images at each spectral band and ten different exposure times to adapt dynamic range and avoid saturated or noisy areas
Reflectance calculation from raw spectral images using calibration images (neutral reference and dark current) to correct for illumination and sensor variations

Lesion segmentation using two different algorithms depending on the spectral range: manual segmentation coupled with image registration for exNIR images, and Otsu's thresholding method on dark level (DL) histograms for VIS-NIR images
Image registration to correlate exNIR images with corresponding VIS range images using mathematical transformation of image matrices
Application of binary masks derived from segmentation to all spectral images to isolate lesion pixels from surrounding healthy skin
conclusions: The developed extended near-infrared (exNIR) multispectral imaging system using an InGaAs sensor (995–1613 nm) successfully provided spectral and spatial information from deeper skin layers, which is valuable for distinguishing between benign and malignant skin lesions.

The classification method combining first-order statistics descriptors, principal component analysis (PCA), and support vector machine (SVM) achieved a sensitivity of 78.6% and a specificity of 84.6% in discriminating between melanomas and nevi.
The specificity of 84.6% obtained with the InGaAs-based system was improved compared to that offered by previous silicon sensor-based systems.
The use of the visible and near-infrared (VIS-NIR) system (414–995 nm) allowed for more accurate localization of lesions with blurry boundaries in the infrared range.

Mean reflectance of melanomas was lower than that of nevi in both VIS-NIR and exNIR ranges, with nevi showing especially higher reflectance from 995 nm to 1350 nm, corresponding to the second near-infrared window where radiation penetrates deeper into tissue.
Nevi were observed to be more homogeneous at all wavelengths, while melanomas grew deeper in the skin, as evidenced by IR images.

The study confirms that exNIR imaging can release information about deeper tissue structures, such as angiogenesis, which may differ between benign and malignant lesions due to factors like water content and ultraviolet radiation damage.

Although the system showed promise, the authors note that multispectral imaging systems have not yet superseded histological examination, which remains the clinical gold standard for diagnostic confirmation.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a small sample size (53 lesions: 39 nevi, 14 melanomas), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is biased towards Caucasian patients from a single center (Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona), raising concerns about external validity for diverse skin types.
No separate training, validation, or testing splits were reported; the entire cohort was used for development and evaluation, increasing the risk of overfitting or optimistic bias.

The classification algorithm relied on iterative thresholding to select features, which introduces subjectivity and potential data leakage if thresholds were optimized on the same data used for final evaluation.
The system has not yet superseded histological examination, which remains the clinical gold standard for diagnostic confirmation.
InGaAs sensors used for exNIR imaging tend to be noisier than CMOS or CCD sensors, and deeper scattering at exNIR wavelengths can reduce image contrast.
Manual segmentation was required for exNIR images, which may introduce inter-observer variability and limit automation potential.
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to support the statistical significance of the observed improvements in specificity.
